EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds enhanced by an upper short wave passing over northern CA/NV, and hot temperatures added to very dry conditions across northern Washoe and northwest Pershing Counties. Gusts reaching up into the 30-40 mph range easily ablated fine dust particles over large areas of the Black Rock that reduced visibility to less than half a mile over Black Rock Desert during the 2022 Burning Man festivities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Burners push their bikes through Saturday's dust storm at Burning Man in Nevada's Black Rock Desert on Sept. 3, 2022.  Andy Barron/Reno Gazzette.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong thunderstorms with concentrated heavy precipitation cores that initiated along the eastern Sierra caused periods of flash flooding and debris flows in and around the Holbrook Junction area that also closed  Nevada SR167 (Highland Way) to the Cal-Neva state line as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused water to flood State Route-359 on the Nevada side.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the final week of December, a very active weather set up with a series of generally warmer Pacific winter storms moving through the region. These winter storms were able to tap into a deep layer of subtropical moisture provided by attendant atmospheric rivers that pushed snow levels higher as compared to earlier storm systems.  The initial winter storm from 26-27 December pushed snow levels mainly above 7000 feet where 1-2 feet of snow fell.  Sierra locations below 7000 feet generally received only 1-6 inches of accumulated snow. Precipitation across much of the rest of western Nevada fell as rain with 0.50-1 inch rainfall totals measured around the Reno-Sparks-Carson City multiplex, which continued south into Douglas and Lyon counties. Lesser precipitation amounts were reported farther east. Strong wind gusts were also reported on the 27 and 30 December, especially in Mineral, Lyon, and southern Churchill counties where weather spotters and the public reported some tree damage. Wind gusts over 90 mph near Walker Lake blew over several tractor-trailers along US-95 while peak gusts across Sierra ridge-tops exceeded 150 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement located 11 SSW of Fallon Station reported an overturned semi-trailer on US-95 near the entrance to the Top Gun raceway due to high winds in the area.. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Twenty miles south of Emigrant Gap, CA. Timber, brush and hardwood litter. Extreme fire behavior with running, spotting and torching. Numerous structures threatened. Evacuations, road, area and trail closures were in effect. 78 structures were lost.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread dense smoke and hazardous air quality impacted the Reno and Carson City and Minden areas much of this week. Low visibility and poor air quality resulted in several cancelled outdoor events.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm, moist southerly flow drawing north into the eastern Sierra, coupled with cooling aloft accompanying a weak negatively tilted shortwave trough lifting northeast across CA/NV destabilized areas south of US 50, and initiated the afternoon development of thunderstorms over the eastern Sierra. Increasing daytime heating and steepening lapse-rates allowed late afternoon/early evening thunderstorm activity to increase later over the Sierra Front, Tahoe Basin, and far western Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Live public webcam located 12 mi NE of Gerlach, NV showed near zero visibility from blowing dust along with strong winds at Black Rock City from nearby showers and thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strongest winter storm of December passed through the region on the final two days of December. This storm initially produced very high snow levels near 9000 feet on 30 December.  Colder air and enhanced forcing, coupled with the final push of extensive moisture worked in unison to rapidly drop snow levels to most valley floors (above 4000 feet) during the morning of New Years Eve. Although this early change-over to snow reduced the flooding risks, it brought widespread winter travel hazards region-wide. Storm total snow totals ranged from 5��� to around 1 foot for lower elevations of western NV/northeast CA with lesser amounts below 4000 feet in west-central Nevada and areas near the Oregon border. Some foothill areas received 1-2 feet plus with Sierra snowfall totals generally ranging from 18-30 inches below 7000 feet. Higher Sierra areas and passes received 3-4 feet plus above 7000 feet. Liquid equivalent totals were impressive with 4-8��� in the Tahoe basin/parts of eastern CA and over 10��� near the Sierra crest. The Reno Airport���s liquid total of 2.26��� on the 31st was the third wettest calendar day on record.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nevada Department of Transportation (NDOT) reported multiple overturned semi-trailer trucks with multiple injuries due to high winds along US-95 near mile marker 55.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP Observer from Moorman Ranch reported 24-hour rainfall at his location of 2.94 inches. He added between 16 and 1800LT a portion of his mountain/hillside came down due to the heavy rain, and he had to clear/repair his driveway with his excavator. Some additional details of note; of the 2.94 inches that fell that Friday, 0.54 inches fell between 2000 that evening and 0400LT the next morning. It was the most rain our observer has seen in a 24-hour period in his 30 years in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep layer of monsoonal moisture pushed northwest and north into California, Sierra, and western Nevada in a southerly upper flow that marked the western margins of an area of strong high-pressure ridging into the Great Basin region.  Thickening mid-level moisture coupled with steep afternoon lapse rates and warmer surface heating initiated deep columns of slow-moving moisture-laden showers and thunderstorms with torrential rains over the 2021 Tamarack burn scar. This resulted in widespread flash flooding and debris flows in Alpine County CA that created a number of impacts. Rainfall rates of 2.22 inches per hour were observed during the peak of the storm in Markleeville, CA which corresponds to at least a 500 year recurrence interval and possibly 1000 year, per NOAA 14 atlas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NDOT and CHP reported mud, dirt, and rock in roadway making Jackson Ranch Road impassible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well-forecast extreme downslope wind event impacted W. Nevada and NE. California with widespread wind gusts between 60 to 95 mph along Highway 395 and I-580 corridor. Several critical highways and interstates were impacted, which disrupted ground travel across the region with strong gusty winds and blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NV Highway Patrol posted on social media @nevadadotreno road closure notice that started at 7pm for I-580 from Bowers,NV to Eastlake,NV in both directions. US-395A will was closed in Washoe Valley at 7pm as well. NHP would reassess at 7am Tuesday (1/4/2022) morning whether to reopen roads. Winds were expected to be in excess of 60mph through the evening which was accompanied with blowing snow. NHP warned to avoid the area if possible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong pressure and thermal gradients tighten across eastern Sierra following cold-frontal (Inside Slider) passage across the region. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet site (SLIDE) located on Slide Mountain, NV reported a wind gust of 72 mph at 0820 LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another inside slider tracked through the region W/more snow and wind.  This inside slider-type system moved into the region late Sunday through Tuesday. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fatal vehicle crash occurred the morning of Tuesday, February 22, around 6:00 am PST involving two vehicles. Details about the crash can be found at this link: https://www.kolotv.com/2022/02/22/major-vehicle-accident-closing-vista-boulevard-both-directions-sparks/.  A slider-type weather system moved over the region late Sunday, February 20, into the morning of Tuesday, February 22, producing a couple rounds of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Breezy northerly winds behind an exiting cold front spread colder continental air (temps overnight dropped to single digits to teens) into the region. This colder air mass resulted in higher snow rations (1:15-1:20) that produced lighter/powdery snow more prone to blowing and/or drifting.  Surface water temperatures also ranged 36��-41��F over the exposed water areas in the days preceding the event. With the marginal CAPE and higher low-level lapse rates, lake effect snow amounts as high as 9-12 swept SW and S over affected areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public in Sparks, NV north to Spanish Springs, NV reported 24hr heavy snowfall over their respective areas, with 5-7��� of snow in and around Sparks with snowfall amounts at various points E and NE of Spanish Springs, NV  ranging 6���-12���.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A third but warmer Pacific spring storm brought lower elevation rain showers, mountain snow, and strong winds to the region the evening of April 15 through April 16. Higher snow levels starting around 7000 feet that lowered to near 6000 feet later on April 16 and brought travel impacts over Sierra passes.  April 16 saw fairly strong winds return to region with the potential for gusts exceeding 50 mph and areas of patchy blowing dust over parts of the northern Washoe County as well as the Basin and Range north of US-50.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NV DOT-mesonet station (WLKNV) located 6.1 mi NW of Hawthorne, NV reported at peak wind gust of 87.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stronger winter storm brought another round of heavy snow, strong winds, and travel impacts to Sierra passes 9-11 December. Peak snowfall totals occurred through the period of 10-11 December with 2-4 feet of snow widely reported in Sierra locations and northeast CA.  Storm total snowfall amounts even exceeded 5 feet over parts of the Sierra crest. Several inches of new snow also fell over some western Nevada valleys with a foot plus falling across some eastern Sierra foothill areas as well.  Strong winds having gusts 65-75 mph in far western NV resulted in reports of damage across parts of Sparks during the early morning of 10 Dec.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public (retired NWS employee) relayed photos of glass bathroom tiles being blown off. Another report included photos that showed wood fence damage as well as several uprooted small trees. Exact time and location estimated from the report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cutoff low lifting into the western US initiated a flow of moderate monsoonal moisture north-northwest along the Sierra crest. Daytime heating, coupled with increased instability and lift from the incoming area of low pressure, touched-off showers, and thunderstorms over the Sierra and parts of western Nevada. A few storms produced localized heavy rain, lightning, and gusty storm outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (CW8738-Reno) located 9.0 mi SSW of Sutcliffe, NV reported a thunderstorm wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed ahead of an upper trough deepening off the Pacific Coast brought strong winds area-wide, along with a chance for light showers and cooler temperatures to the region. Winds were the primary impact as thermal gradients tightened along the cold front with an accompanying increase in upper winds from a strong 100+ knot jet core that lifted north-northeast over the region. This setup increased confidence for moderate travel, recreation, and even fire weather impacts over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (SLIDE), Mount Rose summit (DRI), located 0.9 mi E of the Mount Rose Summit area reported a peak wind gust of 81 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Residual mid-level moisture and intense daytime heating produced high-based showers and virga over the parts of western NV capable of producing gusty downburst winds over the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second much stronger and colder early winter storm system brought more heavy snowfall to the Sierra late Sunday night through midweek.  A much weaker low-pressure system preceded this main event winter storm Saturday through Sunday.  A couple of waves of moderate to heavy snowfall followed and brought travel impacts to region passes through Tuesday. Lower-elevation snow showers then moved into the region Monday morning and became more widespread through Tuesday. Snow showers continued through Wednesday as the central low ejected further east across the Great Basin bringing only minor accumulations to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the Mt. Rose Ski base reported 9.0 inches of new snowfall in the last 24 hours with a storm total of 15 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The western periphery of high-pressure centered over the Four-Corners region contributed to a  surge of mid-level moisture into the Sierra and western NV.  This pattern increased thunderstorm chances south of US-50 with the primary hazards being gusty/erratic outflow boundary winds to 50 mph, patchy dense blowing dust, lightning, and brief heavy rainfall that caused some flash flooding in Mono and Mineral Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water on roadway - on US-95 in both directions near Garfield Flat Road in Mineral County, NV. Single lane alternating traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This is the second of three entries documenting the long-duration excessive heat episode which lasted from August 30th through September 8th. There were 11 heat-related deaths in Las Vegas during the episode; one is documented in the August 30-31 entry, eight are documented in this entry, and two are documented in the September 5-8 entry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This event began in August. Excessive Heat Warning criteria were reached on each of the first four days of September, and the event continued beyond.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple areas reported flooding over roadways, along with some material that traveled with it between the afternoon of the 10th and the morning of the 11th, due to heavy rains in multiple areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 1.5 to 2 mile stretch of road was closed in Lamoille Canyon after a couple of debris flows occurred depositing fine sand, mud, and stones on the roadway. The road was closed for 3 hours for cleanup. The BURN2 RAWS station placed last summer near the Thomas Canyon Campground in Lamoille Canyon measured 0.57 inches of rain in 1 hour, from 15:45 LT to 16:45 LT. At 17:42LT a shift log entry referenced a phone call from a Forest Service representative that said there were some debris flows and that the road was closed. It was reopened at 20:45LT after crews cleared the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple areas reported flooding over roadways, along with some material that traveled with it between the afternoon of the 10th and the morning of the 11th, due to heavy rains in multiple areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NV Roads website reported adverse driving conditions due to a mudslide caused by heavy rain in the area. The slide occurred between mile markers 21 and 24 on NV-722, SW of the city of Austin, in Lander County. Both sides of the highway were affected. The roadway was not closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple areas reported flooding over roadways, along with some material that traveled with it between the afternoon of the 10th and the morning of the 11th, due to heavy rains in multiple areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The MIC at the Elko office reported that 1 to 2 miles east of the South Fork Reservoir, a wash on the east side of the lake filled and overflowed. It was also noted that the overflowing water picked up some debris or had some mixed in it. The water overflowed onto the road for a few inches of depth. Additionally, it was noted that the road that goes around the lake has a steep cliff, and mud and dirt came down the cliff onto the road. This occurred inside a Flood Advisory that was in effect for the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple locations reported a combination of brief heavy rain, flooding, or debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A USGS Hydrologist relayed through dispatch at approximately 19:15LT that there was a minor debris flow 1.9 miles above the Thomas Canyon Campground. The BURN2 station located at Thomas Canyon Campground recorded 0.15 in the previous 3 hours. Although the Lamoille #3 SNOTEL reported over 1 inch for the past 6 hours, with 0.7 inches reported ending at 1500LT and an additional 0.5 for the hour ending at 1600LT, although SNOTELs only report to the nearest tenth of an inch. Additionally, neither MRMS nor Dual Pol had been reporting anything significant over the Rubies since 1600LT. Although there was some debris on the road, the material was small enough that driving through it was manageable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant Thunderstorms with storm motions opposite of cell synoptic flow allowed for slow-moving, training storms. This led to areas of flooding and debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the evening of August 12th, Lamoille Canyon received some brief periods of heavy rainfall. The BURN2 site located at the Thomas Canyon Campground in Lamoille Canyon recorded 0.34 inches that fell over a 15-minute span from 2100 to 2115LT. The area continued to get some more rain and ended up with 0.49 inches in 1 hour from 2100 to 2200 LT. Shortly thereafter a debris flow was reported in the area by a hydrologist from the USGS. Some water, mud, silt, and small rocks washed over the road depositing the silt and mud on the roadway. The hydrologist reiterated that 0.25 inches for 15 minutes or 1 inch/hr seem to be a good threshold for debris flows to occur.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cutoff low lifting into the western US initiated a flow of moderate monsoonal moisture north-northwest along the Sierra crest. Daytime heating, coupled with increased instability and lift from the incoming area of low pressure, touched-off showers, and thunderstorms over the Sierra and parts of western Nevada. A few storms produced localized heavy rain, lightning, and gusty storm outflows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 69 MPH thunderstorm outflow wind gust was measured at the Buffalo Creek RAWS station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cool and wet pattern will persist through mid-week as an upper level low remains stalled off the central California coast. EVENT_NARRATIVE: CO-OP observer station (INVN2), located 1.2 mi E of Incline Village, along with Mesonet stations KNXN2 (2.0 mi N of Incline Village) and EW1336 (2.0 mi NNW of Diamond Peak Ski Resort) reported heavy rainfall amounts of 0.87, 0.92, and 0.86 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong thunderstorms with concentrated heavy precipitation cores that initiated along the eastern Sierra caused periods of flash flooding and debris flows in and around the Holbrook Junction area that also closed  Nevada SR167 (Highland Way) to the Cal-Neva state line as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfalls lead to water over the road near US-395 and Spring Valley drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical moisture associated with the remnants of Hurricane Kay streamed north-northeast across the Sierra and western NV and  resulted in areas of heavy raifall from showers forming across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (NEMNV-NDOT), located 4.6 mi S  of Gold Hill, NV, reported a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep layer of monsoonal moisture pushed northwest and north into California, Sierra, and western Nevada in a southerly upper flow that marked the western margins of an area of strong high-pressure ridging into the Great Basin region.  Thickening mid-level moisture coupled with steep afternoon lapse rates and warmer surface heating initiated deep columns of slow-moving moisture-laden showers and thunderstorms with torrential rains over the 2021 Tamarack burn scar. This resulted in widespread flash flooding and debris flows in Alpine County CA that created a number of impacts. Rainfall rates of 2.22 inches per hour were observed during the peak of the storm in Markleeville, CA which corresponds to at least a 500 year recurrence interval and possibly 1000 year, per NOAA 14 atlas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: It was reported on August 5 by a Washoe County official that flash flooding occurring on August 4 closed the lower Whites Creek trail in Galena Creek Park from Timberline Road to Thomas Creek Road due to extensive trail damage from recent flooding. It was also reported that rainfall amounts between 2.5 and 3 inches occurred in the area on Thursday 8/4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Anomalously strong high pressure built across the season pushed thermometers across the region 10-20 degrees above seasonal averages that broke several high-temperature records across parts of California and Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total number of days in September with at least 100 degrees: 8 days. Prior to 2022, only 8 days reached or exceeded 100 degrees in September through the ENTIRE climate history of Reno, which goes back to 1888. This far exceeded the previous record of 3   days in September 2017 with highs at least 100��F.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Warmest September Temperature: 106��F. This surpassed the highest temperature ever reported in September (and even August), and was equal to the 4th hottest day in  Reno`s climate history (only surpassed by three days with 108��F on July 10-11, 2002 and July 5, 2007). Warmest minimum temperature for any September day: 69��F on  the 7th, surpassing the previous record of 67��F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cool and wet pattern will persist through mid-week as an upper level low remains stalled off the central California coast. An anomalously deep low pressure system yield periods of light to moderate precipitation with a few isolated thunderstorms through Wednesday before the low ejects 19-20 Sept before ejecting northeast 21-22 Sept into the northern Rockies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet stations SKYN2-Skyland (2.0 mi NNE of Zephyr Cove, NV) and FW9917 (in Glenbrook, NV) reported heavy rainfall amounts of 0.54 and 0.63 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the final week of December, a very active weather set up with a series of generally warmer Pacific winter storms moving through the region. These winter storms were able to tap into a deep layer of subtropical moisture provided by attendant atmospheric rivers that pushed snow levels higher as compared to earlier storm systems.  The initial winter storm from 26-27 December pushed snow levels mainly above 7000 feet where 1-2 feet of snow fell.  Sierra locations below 7000 feet generally received only 1-6 inches of accumulated snow. Precipitation across much of the rest of western Nevada fell as rain with 0.50-1 inch rainfall totals measured around the Reno-Sparks-Carson City multiplex, which continued south into Douglas and Lyon counties. Lesser precipitation amounts were reported farther east. Strong wind gusts were also reported on the 27 and 30 December, especially in Mineral, Lyon, and southern Churchill counties where weather spotters and the public reported some tree damage. Wind gusts over 90 mph near Walker Lake blew over several tractor-trailers along US-95 while peak gusts across Sierra ridge-tops exceeded 150 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet site (HVRBL) Mott weather station, located 2.0 mi S of Heavenly Valley, CA, reported a maximum wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strongest winter storm of December passed through the region on the final two days of December. This storm initially produced very high snow levels near 9000 feet on 30 December.  Colder air and enhanced forcing, coupled with the final push of extensive moisture worked in unison to rapidly drop snow levels to most valley floors (above 4000 feet) during the morning of New Years Eve. Although this early change-over to snow reduced the flooding risks, it brought widespread winter travel hazards region-wide. Storm total snow totals ranged from 5��� to around 1 foot for lower elevations of western NV/northeast CA with lesser amounts below 4000 feet in west-central Nevada and areas near the Oregon border. Some foothill areas received 1-2 feet plus with Sierra snowfall totals generally ranging from 18-30 inches below 7000 feet. Higher Sierra areas and passes received 3-4 feet plus above 7000 feet. Liquid equivalent totals were impressive with 4-8��� in the Tahoe basin/parts of eastern CA and over 10��� near the Sierra crest. The Reno Airport���s liquid total of 2.26��� on the 31st was the third wettest calendar day on record.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (WLKNV-NDOT) located 6.1 mi NW of Hawthorne, NV reported a peak wind gust of 93 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stronger winter storm brought another round of heavy snow, strong winds, and travel impacts to Sierra passes 9-11 December. Peak snowfall totals occurred through the period of 10-11 December with 2-4 feet of snow widely reported in Sierra locations and northeast CA.  Storm total snowfall amounts even exceeded 5 feet over parts of the Sierra crest. Several inches of new snow also fell over some western Nevada valleys with a foot plus falling across some eastern Sierra foothill areas as well.  Strong winds having gusts 65-75 mph in far western NV resulted in reports of damage across parts of Sparks during the early morning of 10 Dec.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storey county emergency manager reported via email that he measured 30 inches of accumulated snow that had fallen the previous 24 hours.

This first round of winter systems brought 2-4 feet of snow and travel impacts to higher elevations of the Sierra, a few inches up to 1 foot in and around the Lake Tahoe basin and eastern Sierra foothills of western NV.  Up to 2��� accumulated snow reached the lower valley floors across western NV.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public at Mount Rose Ski Base (8200 feet elevation) reported a 48 hour snowfall of 23 inches with a 24 hour snowfall total of 5 inches.

Some foothill areas received 1-2 feet plus with Sierra snowfall totals generally ranging from 18-30 inches below 7000 feet. Higher Sierra areas and passes received 3-4 feet plus above 7000 feet. Liquid equivalent totals were impressive with 4-8��� in the Tahoe basin/parts of eastern CA and over 10��� near the Sierra crest. The Reno Airport���s liquid total of 2.26��� on the 31st was the third wettest calendar day on record.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet Station (NCVA) NW Minden located 3.0 mi ESE of of Genoa, NV, and mesonet station (NSPO) located at Spooner Summit, NV, reported rainfall totals of 2.37 inches and 2.19 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the final week of December, a very active weather set up with a series of generally warmer Pacific winter storms moving through the region. These winter storms were able to tap into a deep layer of subtropical moisture provided by attendant atmospheric rivers that pushed snow levels higher as compared to earlier storm systems.  The initial winter storm from 26-27 December pushed snow levels mainly above 7000 feet where 1-2 feet of snow fell.  Sierra locations below 7000 feet generally received only 1-6 inches of accumulated snow. Precipitation across much of the rest of western Nevada fell as rain with 0.50-1 inch rainfall totals measured around the Reno-Sparks-Carson City multiplex, which continued south into Douglas and Lyon counties. Lesser precipitation amounts were reported farther east. Strong wind gusts were also reported on the 27 and 30 December, especially in Mineral, Lyon, and southern Churchill counties where weather spotters and the public reported some tree damage. Wind gusts over 90 mph near Walker Lake blew over several tractor-trailers along US-95 while peak gusts across Sierra ridge-tops exceeded 150 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (WLKNV-NDOT) located 6.1 mi NW of Hawthorne, NV reported a peak wind gust of 79 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm generated a series of four waves that brought repeated periods of upper forcing, moisture, and significant snowfall to the higher elevations of the Sierra and western NV from 1-5 December. This first round of winter systems brought 2-4 feet of snow and travel impacts to higher elevations of the Sierra, a few inches up to 1 foot in and around the Lake Tahoe basin and eastern Sierra foothills of western NV.  Up to 2��� accumulated snow reached the lower valley floors across western NV.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (GLCNV) located 2.0 mi NNE of Washoe City (NV DOT) in Washoe County reported a peak wind gust of 71 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strongest winter storm of December passed through the region on the final two days of December. This storm initially produced very high snow levels near 9000 feet on 30 December.  Colder air and enhanced forcing, coupled with the final push of extensive moisture worked in unison to rapidly drop snow levels to most valley floors (above 4000 feet) during the morning of New Years Eve. Although this early change-over to snow reduced the flooding risks, it brought widespread winter travel hazards region-wide. Storm total snow totals ranged from 5��� to around 1 foot for lower elevations of western NV/northeast CA with lesser amounts below 4000 feet in west-central Nevada and areas near the Oregon border. Some foothill areas received 1-2 feet plus with Sierra snowfall totals generally ranging from 18-30 inches below 7000 feet. Higher Sierra areas and passes received 3-4 feet plus above 7000 feet. Liquid equivalent totals were impressive with 4-8��� in the Tahoe basin/parts of eastern CA and over 10��� near the Sierra crest. The Reno Airport���s liquid total of 2.26��� on the 31st was the third wettest calendar day on record.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sierra power outages region wide with power out for tens of thousands of customers.  Due to heavy wet snow loading on area trees, tree limbs and branches broke across and cut power lines across the region. NV Energy announced that 120,000 customers were impacted by power outages caused by snapped trees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast and social media showed areas where heavy wet snow that fell over many areas below 5000 feet elevation caused many travel impacts and contributed to hazardous travel conditions region-wide. Many cars were stranded in upper foothill neighborhoods due to snow depths of several inches over many of those areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stronger winter storm brought another round of heavy snow, strong winds, and travel impacts to Sierra passes 9-11 December. Peak snowfall totals occurred through the period of 10-11 December with 2-4 feet of snow widely reported in Sierra locations and northeast CA.  Storm total snowfall amounts even exceeded 5 feet over parts of the Sierra crest. Several inches of new snow also fell over some western Nevada valleys with a foot plus falling across some eastern Sierra foothill areas as well.  Strong winds having gusts 65-75 mph in far western NV resulted in reports of damage across parts of Sparks during the early morning of 10 Dec.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public at Mount Rose Ski Base, Diamond Peak Ski Base, and a trained spotter located 1.0 mi NNW of Incline Village, NV, reported a 24 hour snowfall totals of 48, 42, and 38 inches respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strongest winter storm of December passed through the region on the final two days of December. This storm initially produced very high snow levels near 9000 feet on 30 December.  Colder air and enhanced forcing, coupled with the final push of extensive moisture worked in unison to rapidly drop snow levels to most valley floors (above 4000 feet) during the morning of New Years Eve. Although this early change-over to snow reduced the flooding risks, it brought widespread winter travel hazards region-wide. Storm total snow totals ranged from 5��� to around 1 foot for lower elevations of western NV/northeast CA with lesser amounts below 4000 feet in west-central Nevada and areas near the Oregon border. Some foothill areas received 1-2 feet plus with Sierra snowfall totals generally ranging from 18-30 inches below 7000 feet. Higher Sierra areas and passes received 3-4 feet plus above 7000 feet. Liquid equivalent totals were impressive with 4-8��� in the Tahoe basin/parts of eastern CA and over 10��� near the Sierra crest. The Reno Airport���s liquid total of 2.26��� on the 31st was the third wettest calendar day on record.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nevada Department of Transportation (NDOT) reported multiple overturned semi-trailer trucks with multiple injuries due to high winds along US-95 near mile marker 55. Mesonet station located at US-95 Walker Lake (WLKNV) reported a max wind gust of 94 mph at 16:01 PST, 12/30/2022.
